content.text:
Knowledge Representation and Reasoning
Semester:
2  |
Type:
UEM2  |
VHS:
63h  |
Weekly hours:
C: 1h30 / TD: 1h30 / TP: 1h30  |
Personal work VHS:
42h  |
Coefficient:
2  |
Credits:
4
Teaching Objectives
Understand the requirements of knowledge representation
Use knowledge representation languages such as RDF, RDFS, OWL and query languages such as SPARQL
Understand the logic behind representation techniques and reasoning to derive new knowledge
Put acquired knowledge into practice
Recommended prior knowledge:
Mathematical logic, foundations of Artificial Intelligence, programming, databases.
Course Content (Lectures: 21h)
Introduction to Knowledge Representation
Definition and challenges: what is knowledge representation? Interests and challenges
Historical development and classification of main approaches and paradigms
Application domains: AI, databases, expert systems, etc.
Description Logics
Propositional Logic: syntax, semantics, decision methods (truth tables, normal forms)
Predicate Logic: syntax, semantics, quantification, unification
Inference: natural deduction, resolution, forward/backward chaining
Knowledge representation in logic: modelling concepts, relations, rules
Symbolic Knowledge Representation
Semantic networks: structure, relationship types, network-based inference
Conceptual graphs and Sowa graphs
Ontologies: structure, formalisms (OWL, RDF), modelling tools (Protégé)
Rule-Based Systems: production rules, inference engine, forward/backward chaining
Frames and Scripts: representation of objects, situations and their behaviours
Management and querying of knowledge graphs with SPARQL
Probabilistic Model-Based Representation
Probability: foundations, Bayes' theorem, conditional probabilities
Bayesian Networks: structure, learning, inference
Markov chains and hidden Markov models: representation and inference in sequences
Applications to speech recognition and time series analysis
Knowledge Representation for the Semantic Web
Resource Description Framework (RDF): data structure, graph model, triples
Web Ontology Language (OWL): vocabulary, classes, properties, inference
Linked Open Data: principles, best practices, linked open data
Assessment
In-person semester exam (60%)
Continuous assessment (CC) (40%): written tests, personal work, tests
Practical work uses:
Protégé, TopBraid Composer modelling tools; Prolog and Lisp programming languages; expert systems, decision support and information retrieval case studies.
References
Ronald J. Brachman et Hector J. Levesque (2004).
Knowledge representation and reasoning
. Morgan Kaufmann Publishers Inc.
Franz Baader, Ian Horrocks, Carsten Lutz, Uli Sattler (2017).
An Introduction to Description Logic
. Cambridge University Press.
John F. Sowa (2000).
Principles of Knowledge Representation and reasoning
. Morgan Kaufmann.
Dean Allemang, Jim Hendler, Fabien Gandon (2020).
Semantic Web for the Working Ontologist
. Third edition, ACM Books.
Stuart Russell et Peter Norvig (2020).
Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach
.
Bob DuCharme (2013).
Learning SPARQL
. Second Edition, O'Reilly.
